curriculum.subject: CHEMISTRY
curriculum.overallDescription: 10 units, 9 chapters, and 0 topics extracted through raw extraction, document-structure hierarchy building, node enrichment, normalized teaching blocks, validation, and statistics generation.
coreObjectives: Understand the importance of chemistry in daily life and career paths.
Distinguish between nature of matter and its properties.
Apply principles of measurement and handle uncertainty correctly.
Utilize Laws of Chemical Combination to predict reaction outcomes.
Explain Dalton's Atomic Theory and its implications for atomic/molecular masses.
Perform mole concept calculations, molar mass determinations, and percentage composition analysis.
Identify and describe the discovery of sub-atomic particles.
Compare historical atomic models and their limitations.
Analyze developments leading to Bohr's Model for Hydrogen Atom.
Apply Quantum Mechanical concepts to explain modern atomic structure.
Justify the need for classifying elements and trace the genesis of periodic classification.

units.unit_name: Practical Examination for Visually Challenged Students Class XI
topics: Characterization and Purification of Chemical Substances: Crystallization of an impure sample (copper sulphate, benzoic acid)
Experiments based on pH: Determination of pH of solutions from fruit juices/known concentrations; Comparing pH of strong and weak acids
Chemical Equilibrium: Shift in equilibrium between ferric ions/thiocyanate ions; Shift in equilibrium between [Co(H2O)6] and chloride ions
Quantitative estimation: Preparation of standard solution of oxalic acid; Determination of molarity of sodium hydroxide by titration against oxalic acid
Qualitative Analysis: Determination of one anion and one cation (NH4+, CO3, S, SO3, Cl, CH3COO); Detection of Nitrogen in organic compound; Detection of Halogen in organic compound

Unit 3: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties - 6 Marks
Unit 4: Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure - 7 Marks
Unit 8: Organic Chemistry: Some basic Principles and Techniques - 11 Marks
question_paper_design: Note on Unit 8 and 9 topics (s & p Block Elements, The Gaseous State): Assessed only formatively without adding to summative assessments.
internal_assessment: Viva: Included in Class record and viva (Total 30 for practicals)

practical_assessment.visually_challenged_students_scheme: Same evaluation scheme and general guidelines as Class XII.

project_assessment: Suggested Projects Assessed Formatively:
- Checking the bacterial contamination in drinking water by testing sulphide ion

- Investigation of foaming capacity of different washing soaps and effect of Sodium carbonate addition
- Study acidity of different samples of tea leaves
- Determination of rate of evaporation of different liquids
- Study effect of acids and bases on tensile strength of fibers
- Study acidity of fruit and vegetable juices

competency_groups.title: Some Basic Concepts of Chemistry
competency_groups.description: Foundational concepts including matter, measurement laws, atomic theory, and stoichiometry.
competencies: Understand the importance of chemistry in daily life and career paths.
Distinguish between nature of matter and its properties.
Apply principles of measurement and handle uncertainty correctly.
Utilize Laws of Chemical Combination to predict reaction outcomes.
Explain Dalton's Atomic Theory and its implications for atomic/molecular masses.
Perform mole concept calculations, molar mass determinations, and percentage composition analysis.
Execute stoichiometric calculations accurately.

competency_groups.title: Structure of Atom
competency_groups.description: Evolution from sub-atomic particles to quantum mechanical models.
competencies: Identify and describe the discovery of sub-atomic particles.
Compare historical atomic models and their limitations.
Analyze developments leading to Bohr's Model for Hydrogen Atom.
Apply Quantum Mechanical concepts to explain modern atomic structure.

competency_groups.title: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
competency_groups.description: Understanding periodic classification, electronic configurations, and trends.
competencies: Justify the need for classifying elements and trace the genesis of periodic classification.
Apply Modern Periodic Law to interpret the structure of the Periodic Table.
Use IUPAC nomenclature rules for elements with atomic number > 100.
Correlate electronic configuration with element types (s, p, d, f blocks).
Predict and explain periodic trends in elemental properties.
competency_groups.unit_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
competency_groups.chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

competency_groups.title: Chemical Bonding and Molecular Structure
competency_groups.description: Mechanisms of bond formation, theories of bonding, and molecular geometry.
competencies: Apply Kossel-Lewis approach to explain chemical bonding.
Differentiate between ionic/electrovalent bonds and covalent interactions.
Analyze bond parameters (length, energy, angle) in molecules.
Predict molecular geometry using VSEPR Theory.
Explain hybridization concepts and Valence Bond Theory applications.
Interpret Molecular Orbital Theory for homonuclear diatomic molecules.
Describe the nature and impact of Hydrogen bonding.

competency_groups.title: Thermodynamics
competency_groups.description: Energy changes, spontaneity, and equilibrium in chemical systems.
competencies: Define thermodynamic terms and apply them to real-world applications.
Measure internal energy (∆U) and enthalpy change (∆H) using calorimetry.
Calculate reaction enthalpies for various types of reactions.
Determine the spontaneity of processes based on Gibbs Energy Change.

competency_groups.title: Equilibrium
competency_groups.description: Dynamic equilibrium in physical and chemical processes, including ionic equilibria.
competencies: Distinguish between dynamic equilibrium in physical vs. chemical processes.
Apply the Law of Chemical Equilibrium to calculate equilibrium constants (K).
Differentiate between homogeneous and heterogeneous equilibria.
Relate Reaction Quotient (Q) and Gibbs Energy (G) to K for predicting reaction direction.
Analyze factors affecting chemical equilibria (Le Chatelier's principle applications).
Solve problems involving ionic equilibrium, acid-base ionization, buffer solutions, and solubility products.

competency_groups.title: Redox Reactions
competency_groups.description: Oxidation-reduction concepts, electron transfer, and electrode processes.
competencies: Define oxidation and reduction from classical perspectives (oxygen/hydrogen gain/loss).
Analyze redox reactions in terms of electron transfer mechanisms.
Assign and calculate oxidation numbers to identify oxidizing/reducing agents.
Connect redox concepts to electrode processes.

competency_groups.title: Organic Chemistry: Some basic Principles and Techniques
competency_groups.description: Fundamentals of organic structure, nomenclature, isomerism, mechanisms, and analysis.
competencies: Explain the tetravalence of carbon and shapes of organic compounds.
Represent organic structures using various standard notations.
Classify organic compounds based on functional groups and skeletons.
Apply IUPAC nomenclature rules to name complex organic molecules.
Identify different types of isomerism in organic compounds.
Analyze fundamental concepts of reaction mechanisms (electrophiles, nucleophiles).
Execute methods for purification and qualitative/quantitative analysis of organic substances.
competency_groups.unit_name: Organic Chemistry: Some basic Principles and Techniques

competency_groups.title: Hydrocarbons
competency_groups.description: Classification, properties, and reactions of alkanes, alkenes, alkynes, and aromatics.
competencies: Classify hydrocarbons into saturated (alkanes), unsaturated (alkenes/alkynes), and aromatic types.
Describe the structure and chemical behavior of Alkanes, Alkenes, and Alkynes.
Analyze the properties and stability of Aromatic Hydrocarbons.
Evaluate carcinogenic effects and toxicity associated with specific hydrocarbon classes.

competency_groups.title: Suggested Projects (Formative Assessment)
competency_groups.description: Application of chemical principles through investigative projects and practical analysis.
competencies: Design and execute experiments to test bacterial contamination in water via sulphide ion detection.
Investigate methods for purifying drinking water.
Analyze regional variations in water hardness, Iron, Fluoride, and Chloride content against permissible limits.
Evaluate the foaming capacity of soaps and the effect of Sodium carbonate additives.
Measure acidity levels across different tea leaf samples.
Determine evaporation rates for various liquids under controlled conditions.
Assess the impact of acids/bases on fiber tensile strength.
Quantify acidity in fruit and vegetable juice samples.

- Study of the methods of purification of water
- Testing the hardness, presence of Iron, Fluoride, Chloride, etc., depending upon regional variation and study of causes of presence above permissible limit
- Investigation of foaming capacity of different washing soaps and effect of Sodium carbonate addition
- Study acidity of different samples of tea leaves
- Determination of rate of evaporation of different liquids
- Study effect of acids and bases on tensile strength of fibers
- Study acidity of fruit and vegetable juices
